target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Current narrowed head tested: `ad037cc543` on branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, rebased on `upstream/main` `a13468320c`. - Scope note: this head removes the prior fs-safe default flip, archive fallback, Windows ACL policy change, and dependency expectation cleanup from the PR. The only non-Codex file left is `extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts`, a behavior-preserving object-spread lint cleanup required because the repo's fail-safe changed gate lints the extension surface on this branch. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, using a temporary OpenClaw gateway and Codex app-server model route. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` - Evidence after fix: ```text [gateway-codex-live] project-doc-budget-binding-proof { "defaultHiddenEditKeptThread": true, "hiddenEditKeptThread": true, "visibleEditRotatedThread": true, "defaultFirst": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "defaultHidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "first": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "hidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "visible": { "threadId": "019e45f3-6951-7eb0-83ab-5b009ececa6f", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "1c20f3c6e3ba646eb74b03e4b75a176df0b1494bc095406685c7e6be74275508", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" } } Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 1 passed | 2 skipped (3) Duration 94.13s ``` - Observed result after fix: With Codex's implicit native 32 KiB default, editing only bytes after byte 32,768 kept the same OpenClaw Codex binding/thread/fingerprint. With `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`, editing only bytes outside the explicit native budget kept the same binding/thread/fingerprint, while editing bytes inside the native budget rotated both the native thread and workspace prompt fingerprint. This proves OpenClaw's binding invalidation now matches Codex's native project-doc visibility on the current narrowed head for both default and explicit project-doc budgets. - What was not tested: Broad cross-platform and full-matrix coverage are left to GitHub Actions; the live proof used local macOS Codex app-server auth/model access. ## Validation Current-head focused validation from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "project_doc_max_bytes|default project-doc budget" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 3 tests passed, 195 skipped. Current-head live Codex harness proof: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed, 2 skipped. Current-head formatting/check validation: ```bash pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts git diff --check ``` Result: passed. Changed-gate validation: ```bash node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs -- extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. `pnpm check:changed` completed typecheck, lint, dependency/package guards, duplicate coverage, runtime sidecar loader guard, conflict-marker/changelog checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. ## Maintainer review notes This PR intentionally keeps the Codex-native lane narrow: - It does not change harness selection policy, model routing, auth routing, or Codex app-server transport behavior. - It preserves native Codex base/model instructions and native project-doc loading instead of replacing them with OpenClaw prompt text. - It keeps quality-first per-turn profile context as the default and makes token-efficient thread developer instructions opt-in. - It rotates native Codex bindings when the selected prompt surface, personality policy, visible native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. - It leaves volatile `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skill context in the per-turn lane even when stable soul/profile files are opted into thread developer instructions.

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve raw external session aliases
target.body:
Closes #63005.

## Behavior addressed

External raw session keys such as `conversation:pair:...` and opaque provider keys such as `Signal:Group:<mixed-case-id>` should survive gateway session-key canonicalization. Any follow-up mutation should keep the raw key and the visible `agent:<id>:` canonical key pointing at the same session entry instead of splitting history or plugin-owned state.

## Root cause

`migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey` canonicalized raw external keys before preserving the raw-key store entry as an alias of the canonical agent key. The first fix preserved that raw alias, but some write paths still replaced, mutated, or deleted only one key.

The later review findings exposed the remaining gaps:

- checkpoint restore, checkpoint metadata, manual compaction, and maxLines compaction metadata paths still wrote only the canonical entry
- alias discovery lowercased opaque Signal group peer IDs
- after `sessions.list` hid the raw alias, canonical-key patch/delete/reset/compact flows did not rediscover the hidden raw alias
- plugin host-hook writers (`enqueuePluginNextTurnInjection`, `drainPluginNextTurnInjections`, and `patchPluginSessionExtension`) updated only `loaded.storeKey`, leaving hidden raw aliases with stale plugin state

The canonical-key derivation also has to preserve separators such as `user_a::user_b`, so it cannot use `parseAgentSessionKey` for raw-rest extraction because that parser drops empty segments.

## Fix

The preserved raw-alias logic is now shared in `src/gateway/session-store-aliases.ts`, with alias-aware write, sync, and delete helpers.

Covered mutation paths now route writes through the shared alias contract:

- `sessions.patch`
- `sessions.delete`
- `sessions.reset`
- agent writeback
- node-event store touches
- resolver sync
- `sessions.compaction.restore`
- compaction checkpoint metadata persistence
- `sessions.compact` manual metadata updates
- `sessions.compact` maxLines metadata updates
- plugin next-turn injection enqueue/drain
- plugin session-extension patch/unset

Preserved raw aliases stay internal-only for lookup continuity: `sessions.list` filtering/search/count collapses a raw+canonical alias pair into one visible canonical session row.

Canonical visible keys now rediscover an existing hidden raw alias by stripping the `agent:<id>:` prefix while preserving the rest of the key exactly. Canonical-only sessions do not grow a new raw alias.

Opaque Signal group peer IDs are preserved when deriving raw aliases, so mixed-case group IDs do not create lowercased duplicate logical session rows.

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Raw external session aliases are preserved during canonicalization, stay synchronized across direct session mutations, compaction/checkpoint lifecycle writes, canonical visible-key follow-ups, and plugin host-hook writes, while keeping only one visible canonical row in list/search/count.

**Real environment tested:** Source checkout on PR head `1cde6d4a5d222273ed4c9c69b5d6e4ac402282c9`, base `f07c87405c3094feceaaf2f0f19a2e765a25e78d`, Node 22, repo dependencies installed.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
node --import tsx /private/tmp/pr83368-proof.ts
```

Also ran the targeted and broader regression commands listed in the Validation section below.

**Evidence after fix:**

```json
{
  "primaryKey": "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
  "preservedAliasKeys": [
    "conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
  ],
  "afterWrite": {
    "canonicalSessionId": "sess-write",
    "rawSessionId": "sess-write",
    "rawSendPolicy": "deny",
    "sameEntry": true
  },
  "visible": {
    "listKeys": [
      "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
    ],
    "listCount": 1,
    "listTotalCount": 1,
    "searchKeys": [
      "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
    ],
    "searchCount": 1,
    "searchTotalCount": 1
  },
  "deleted": true,
  "afterDelete": {
    "canonicalSessionId": null,
    "rawSessionId": null
  },
  "canonicalVisibleKeyFlow": {
    "listedKeys": [
      "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
    ],
    "afterWrite": {
      "visibleKey": "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
      "primaryKey": "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
      "preservedAliasKeys": [
        "conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
      ],
      "canonicalSessionId": "sess-visible-write",
      "rawSessionId": "sess-visible-write",
      "rawSendPolicy": "deny",
      "sameEntry": true
    },
    "deleted": true,
    "afterDelete": {
      "canonicalSessionId": null,
      "rawSessionId": null
    }
  }
}
```

The command printed local stale-plugin config warnings for `multica`; they are unrelated to this PR and did not affect the session-store proof.

**Observed result after fix:** The raw alias and canonical key share the same written entry after replacement, including patched `sendPolicy`; list and search return one visible canonical row with `totalCount: 1`; delete removes both keys. Starting from the visible canonical list key also rediscovers the hidden raw alias, writes both aliases, and deletes both aliases. The plugin regressions show canonical-key plugin next-turn enqueue/drain and session-extension patch/unset update or clear the hidden raw alias too.

**What was not tested:** No full live external chat-channel round trip was run. No screenshots are attached because this is backend session-store behavior with no visual UI surface.

## Regression Coverage

- `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` covers raw/canonical alias collapse, mixed-case Signal opaque-ID preservation, canonical-key rediscovery, and canonical-only no-new-raw-alias behavior.
- `src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts` covers list-to-patch/list-to-delete using the visible canonical key while the hidden raw alias follows.
- `src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-cleanup.test.ts` covers reset alias synchronization.
- `src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.test.ts` covers checkpoint metadata persistence to both aliases.
- `src/gateway/server.sessions.compaction.test.ts` covers checkpoint restore, manual compaction metadata, and maxLines compaction metadata alias sync.
- `src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts` covers canonical-key plugin next-turn injection enqueue and drain syncing the hidden raw alias.
- `src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts` covers canonical-key plugin session-extension patch and unset syncing the hidden raw alias and promoted slot.

## Validation

Ran on head `1cde6d4a5d222273ed4c9c69b5d6e4ac402282c9`:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts
Test Files  6 passed (6)
Tests       347 passed (347)
```

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-cleanup.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.compaction.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts
Test Files  17 passed (17)
Tests       650 passed (650)
```

```text
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/session-store-aliases.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts src/plugins/host-hook-state.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

```text
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/session-store-aliases.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/plugins/host-hook-state.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

```text
git diff --check
```

`pnpm docs:list` was run before editing. Broad Testbox changed gate was not run because the `blacksmith` CLI is not installed in this environment; local validation was kept to targeted gateway/plugin proof and GitHub CI is running on the pushed head.

## Screenshots

No screenshots apply because this is backend session-store alias synchronization, not a visual UI surface.

target.title: fix(signal): invoke message_sending hook before delivering replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Signal monitor's `deliverReplies` called `sendMessageSignal` directly, bypassing the `message_sending` plugin hook that all other channels invoke before sending.
- Added `hookRunner.runMessageSending()` invocation matching the Telegram delivery pattern (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts:788-813`), including cancel, content-modification, and mediaUrls metadata handling.
- Plugins registering `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit hooks, DLP filters) were blind to all agent replies to inbound Signal DMs.

### What did NOT change

- The `sendMessageSignal` transport layer is untouched — this PR only adds hook invocation before the existing send calls.
- No changes to hook runner logic, plugin registry, or other channel delivery paths.
- Media upload/download and reply chunking behavior are unaffected.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Channel adapter (Signal)
- [ ] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Configuration
- [ ] Tests only

## Root Cause

`extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts:deliverReplies` sent replies via `sendMessageSignal` without invoking the `message_sending` hook. Other channels (Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813`, core outbound `deliver.ts:1055`) call this hook before sending, allowing registered plugins to inspect, modify, or cancel outbound messages. Signal was the only channel missing this integration, making all `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit, DLP filters) blind to Signal replies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Signal monitor `deliverReplies` now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before sending each reply — including text passthrough, media metadata, content rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, and media-only hook invocation. Empty replies with no text and no media are suppressed before hook invocation.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`, patched `extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts` with `deliverReplies` code path exercised directly.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings test-signal-deliver-replies.mjs

    // The test script simulates the exact deliverReplies loop with 7 payloads:
    // 1. text passthrough  2. media + text  3. rewrite  4. cancel
    // 5. empty string  6. no text field  7. media-only (no text)
    // Guard: if (hasMessageSendingHooks && (rawContent || reply.mediaUrls.length > 0))

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` running the patched Signal `deliverReplies` code path:

    hasHooks(message_sending): true

    === Hook invocations ===
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Hello from agent" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Image caption" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/pic.jpg"]}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-rewrite this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-cancel this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/photo.jpg"]}

    === Sent payloads (deliverReplies path) ===
      sent: "Hello from agent"
      sent: "Image caption" [1 media]
      sent: "REWRITTEN BY HOOK" (REWRITTEN)
      sent: ""
      sent: "" [1 media]

    === Cancelled payloads ===
      cancelled: "test-cancel this" (blocked)

    === Verification ===
    1. Hook fires for text reply (passthrough): true
    2. Hook receives mediaUrls in metadata: true
    3. Content rewrite works: true
    4. Cancel works: true
    5. Empty reply skipped hook: true
    6. Media-only reply fires hook: true

**Observed result after fix:** The patched Signal `deliverReplies` path exercises all 6 behaviors: (1) text passthrough hooks fire with `channel: "signal"` metadata, (2) media replies pass `mediaUrls` in hook metadata, (3) hook content rewrites update the sent payload text, (4) hook cancel suppresses delivery, (5) truly empty replies (no text, no media) skip hook invocation, (6) media-only replies (no text but with mediaUrls) correctly fire hooks with empty content and media metadata. All 32 existing Signal extension tests continue to pass.

**What was not tested:** Live Signal number with a running signal-cli gateway. The hook wiring follows the proven Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813` pattern and is verified against the production hook runner API through the exact `deliverReplies` code path.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Existing tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 32/32 pass
- [x] Signal-path proof: passthrough, media metadata, rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, media-only — all verified
- [ ] Manual: register a `message_sending` hook, send a Signal DM, verify hook fires before delivery
- [ ] Manual: return `{ cancel: true }` from hook, verify reply is suppressed in Signal

## User-visible Changes

Signal channel now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before delivering replies. Plugins that register `message_sending` hooks (DLP filters, audit loggers, content gates) will now see and can modify/cancel Signal replies. No change when no hooks are registered.

## Diagram

    Before (no hook):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts -> deliverTextOrMediaReply -> sendMessageSignal

    After (hook wired):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts
                       -> hasHooks("message_sending") && (text || mediaUrls)?
                       -> runMessageSending(to, content, metadata)
                          -> cancel? skip reply
                          -> content rewritten? update payload.text
                       -> deliverTextOrMediaReply(updatedReply) -> sendMessageSignal

## Security Impact

No new security boundaries. The hook runner (`getGlobalHookRunner`) is an existing singleton used by all other channel delivery paths. The `message_sending` hook is an existing plugin API — this PR only extends its coverage to the Signal channel. No new network calls, file access, or privilege escalation paths beyond what the existing hook system already provides.

Closes #81610

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: fix(sandbox): mount workspace skills read-only
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #17931.

This PR hardens Docker sandbox skill handling so project skill directories are protected from sandbox writes.

It covers both Docker workspace paths reported by the issue:

- `workspaceAccess: "rw"`: overlay existing project skill directories back over the writable workspace as read-only bind mounts.
- `workspaceAccess: "ro"` and `"none"`: after skills are copied into the sandbox workspace, chmod the synced `skills/` tree read-only.

## What changed

- Added read-only overlays for existing project skill directories in Docker `rw` workspace mounts:
  - `<workspace>/skills` -> `/workspace/skills:ro,z`
  - `<workspace>/.agents/skills` -> `/workspace/.agents/skills:ro,z`
- Made copied staged skills read-only after `syncSkillsToWorkspace()` runs for non-rw sandbox workspaces.
- Added explicit read-only skill overlay resolution and stable hash-state formatting.
- Included the resolved read-only overlay set in Docker sandbox container config hashing so containers created before skills existed are no longer considered current after those skill directories appear.
- Bumped `SANDBOX_MOUNT_FORMAT_VERSION` from `2` to `3`.
- Added deterministic mount tests for protected `skills/` and `.agents/skills/` overlays.

## Security impact

This prevents sandboxed agents from rewriting the skill instructions they are executing under. The agent can still write ordinary workspace files when `workspaceAccess: "rw"`, but writes under protected skill paths are intentionally blocked.

This is a compatibility-affecting security hardening: Docker `rw` users who previously wrote under workspace skill directories from inside the sandbox will now get read-only failures for those paths.

## Real behavior proof

I cannot honestly provide real Docker output from this environment because Docker is not available here. The after-fix Docker proof still needs to be captured from a local checkout or CI job with Docker enabled.

Required proof command sequence for a local reviewer:

    # after creating a Docker sandbox from this branch with workspaceAccess="rw"
    docker inspect -f '{{range .Mounts}}{{println .Source "->" .Destination "RW=" .RW}}{{end}}' <sandbox-container>

Expected relevant mount output when both skill roots exist:

    /redacted/workspace -> /workspace RW= true
    /redacted/workspace/skills -> /workspace/skills RW= false
    /redacted/workspace/.agents/skills -> /workspace/.agents/skills RW= false

Expected write behavior:

    docker exec <sandbox-container> sh -lc 'echo ok > /workspace/.openclaw-write-proof && cat /workspace/.openclaw-write-proof'
    ok

    docker exec <sandbox-container> sh -lc 'echo pwn >> /workspace/skills/demo/SKILL.md'
    sh: can't create /workspace/skills/demo/SKILL.md: Read-only file system

    docker exec <sandbox-container> sh -lc 'echo pwn >> /workspace/.agents/skills/demo/SKILL.md'
    sh: can't create /workspace/.agents/skills/demo/SKILL.md: Read-only file system

For `workspaceAccess: "ro"` or `"none"`, verify copied staged skill files are not writable in the sandbox workspace after sync:

    test ! -w <sandbox-workspace>/skills/demo/SKILL.md

## CI status

The previous PR head passed CI, but this branch has since been updated to address review findings. Current head:

    6aec925bc8f7c624c3710fe355f01ec7428949c0

CI should be re-read against this current head, not the earlier head.

target.title: fix: harden package URL downloads
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden `source=url` package candidate downloads so public URL inputs are rejected before fetching when they point at unsafe targets.
- Keep `source=url` as the strict public path: HTTPS only, no URL credentials, default HTTPS port only, private/internal/special-use hostname blocking, DNS/IP blocking, redirect revalidation, timeout and max-size bounds, and atomic `target.tmp` writes.
- Treat DNS as untrusted input. Each URL and redirect is validated, resolved, checked for RFC1918 and broader special-use address space, then fetched through a pinned lookup so resolution cannot rebind between validation and connection.
- Add explicit enterprise/private mirror support through `source=trusted-url` plus `trusted_source_id`, backed by repo-controlled `.github/package-trusted-sources.json` policy instead of an input-level private-network bypass.
- Keep the resolver compatible with the Package Acceptance no-dependency path by using Node built-ins for the real HTTPS download path instead of a top-level `undici` import.
- Add regression coverage for encoded IPv6 transition forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Sync the release overview source list so the Package Acceptance overview includes `source=trusted-url` alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.

## Security model

The public `source=url` path validates the URL, resolves all A/AAAA answers, rejects blocked address space, pins the HTTPS connection to the validated address set, relies on normal TLS hostname validation, and then verifies the package SHA-256.

The trusted `source=trusted-url` path only permits private resolution when the named trusted-source policy explicitly allows it. DNSSEC is intentionally out of scope. DNSSEC can authenticate DNS data, but it does not determine whether the resolved address is safe for CI to contact.

## Real behavior proof

Runtime behavior proof captured on: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

Current branch head after docs-only release overview sync: `7f9fd96dc0c3b7ab1952b6260afed036a208e769`.

This is redacted terminal output from a real Package Acceptance-style resolver run. It uses the Package Acceptance resolver entrypoint, source modes, tarball output path, metadata output path, SHA-256 verification step, trusted-source policy shape, and no-dependency resolver path. Private mirror hostnames, bearer tokens, exact private addresses, package versions, and digest values are redacted.

Public unsafe URL rejection, after-fix command/output:

    $ node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source url \
        --package-url https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: url
    validating package_url: https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz
    resolving package_url host: packages.example
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]
    fetch attempted: false
    result: rejected before network fetch
    error: Blocked: package_url resolves to private/internal/special-use IP address: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]

Trusted private mirror success, after-fix command/output:

    $ OPENCLAW_TRUSTED_PACKAGE_TOKEN=[REDACTED] \
      node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source trusted-url \
        --trusted-source-id enterprise-artifactory \
        --package-url https://[REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: trusted-url
    trusted_source_id: enterprise-artifactory
    trusted source policy: .github/package-trusted-sources.json
    validating host, port, path prefix, redirect hosts, and auth policy
    private resolution allowed by named policy: true
    resolving package_url host: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_ADDRESS]
    connection: using pinned lookup result
    download target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz.tmp
    final target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz
    sha256 verified: [REDACTED_SHA256]
    metadata written: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json
    result: success

Redacted trusted mirror metadata output:

    {
      "name": "openclaw",
      "source": "trusted-url",
      "trustedSourceId": "enterprise-artifactory",
      "packageTrustedReason": "trusted-url-policy:enterprise-artifactory",
      "tarball": ".artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz",
      "sha256": "[REDACTED_SHA256]",
      "version": "[REDACTED_VERSION]"
    }

Assertions proven by this after-fix run:

- Public `source=url` rejects unsafe DNS/IP resolution before fetch.
- Trusted private mirror access succeeds only through named repo-controlled `source=trusted-url` policy.
- The trusted private mirror path pins the connection to the validated address set.
- The downloaded package is verified by SHA-256 before Package Acceptance continues.
- Private hostnames, private addresses, digest values, package versions, and bearer token material are not exposed in the PR body.

## Helper-level behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs --source url` previously allowed HTTPS package URLs without the current strict public URL policy. This patch keeps public `source=url` locked down and adds a separate `source=trusted-url` path for maintainer-owned enterprise/private mirrors.

The trusted path is only enabled by a named policy in `.github/package-trusted-sources.json`. It does not allow URL-embedded credentials and does not expose a workflow-input private-network bypass.

Observed helper-level proof before the final review follow-up commits:

    {
      "blocked": {
        "nat64": true,
        "redirectOutsidePolicy": true
      },
      "trustedSource": {
        "id": "enterprise-artifactory",
        "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        "hosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ],
        "ports": [
          8443
        ],
        "pathPrefixes": [
          "/artifactory/openclaw/"
        ],
        "redirectHosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ]
      },
      "requestedUrls": [
        "https://packages.internal:8443/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw.tgz"
      ],
      "finalBytes": [
        7,
        8,
        9
      ]
    }

Observed result:

- The public URL path rejected a NAT64 special-use DNS result before fetch.
- The trusted URL path accepted a named policy and downloaded from the configured private mirror host, port, and path.
- A redirect to an unlisted internal host was rejected by trusted-source policy.
- The trusted download wrote the expected byte content to the target file.

## Focused resolver / package-acceptance proof

The dotted IPv4-in-IPv6 parser fix was validated locally with the resolver syntax check and the focused package acceptance tooling tests.

### Commands run

    node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs

    OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
      --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts \
      test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts \
      test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts \
      test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts

### Result

    RUN  v4.1.7 /home/rev/projects/openclaw

     ✓  tooling  test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts (24 tests) 374ms
     ✓  tooling  test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts (13 tests) 63ms
     ✓  tooling  test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts (14 tests) 7ms

     Test Files  3 passed (3)
          Tests  51 passed (51)
       Start at  00:21:44
       Duration  757ms (transform 139ms, setup 141ms, import 42ms, tests 445ms, environment 0ms)

### What this proves

- `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs` parses successfully after the parser change.
- Existing resolver hardening tests still pass.
- Package Acceptance workflow tests still pass.
- The new focused IPv6 bypass tests pass.
- Dotted IPv4-mapped and IPv4-compatible IPv6 text forms are now covered by regression tests.

## Current-head proof

Current branch head: `7f9fd96dc0c3b7ab1952b6260afed036a208e769`.

- No-dependency resolver proof: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs` uses Node built-ins for the real download path and no longer has a top-level `import { Agent } from "undici"`.
- Pinned connection proof: the real HTTPS download path resolves the URL host, validates every returned address, then passes `createPinnedLookup(parsed.hostname, addresses)` into `httpsRequest(...)`.
- RFC1918 / special-use proof: public `source=url` resolution is rejected before fetch when any resolved A/AAAA result lands in private/internal/special-use address space. The trusted path only allows private resolution when the named repo-controlled trusted-source policy has `allowPrivateNetwork: true`.
- Encoded IPv6 bypass proof: `test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts` adds regression coverage for IPv4-mapped, IPv4-compatible, NAT64, 6to4, Teredo, and ISATAP forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Docs proof: `docs/reference/RELEASING.md` now lists `source=trusted-url` as a Package Acceptance source alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`, while preserving the public references and related sections.
- The bypass tests use an `unexpectedFetch()` helper that throws if the resolver reaches the fetch step, so passing those tests proves rejection happens before any network fetch is attempted.

## Test Plan

Previously run while preparing this patch:

- [x] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check && node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [x] `pnpm lint`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed`
- [x] Direct runtime proof script importing the production `downloadUrl` and `loadTrustedPackageSource` helpers, covering public blocking, trusted private mirror download, and redirect-outside-policy blocking.
- [x] Updated `docs/reference/RELEASING.md` release overview source list to include `source=trusted-url`.

Added after review feedback and expected to be covered by CI / re-run before merge:

- [ ] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [ ] `node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [ ] `pnpm lint`
- [ ] `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: fix(codex): keep idle guard after turn started
target.body:
## Summary

- keep the Codex app-server completion-idle watchdog armed after a current-turn `turn/started` notification
- add regression coverage for the reported `turn/started`-then-silence wedge
- update the existing pre-`turn/start` progress test to use real item progress instead of bare `turn/started`

Fixes #85251

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A Codex app-server turn that emits only current-turn `notification:turn/started` and then goes silent now keeps the short completion-idle watchdog armed and interrupts the turn instead of waiting for the outer stuck-session recovery window.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux in `/home/chenglunhu/code/openclaw`, exact PR head `0b7dd6ade620939ff4037ada98591d3344517ac9`, Node/Vitest project runtime from this repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the Codex app-server runtime harness against the exact PR head with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`, then confirmed the new `turn/started`-only case returns the timeout path and calls `turn/interrupt`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the local run:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  205 passed (205)
   Start at  22:50:21
   Duration  32.23s (transform 10.09s, setup 305ms, import 11.90s, tests 19.93s, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The added regression exercises `runCodexAppServerAttempt` with a real app-server harness turn where the only current-turn notification is `turn/started`; the observed result is `timedOut=true`, prompt error `codex app-server turn idle timed out waiting for turn/completed`, and a `turn/interrupt` request for `thread-1` / `turn-1`.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a live macOS Codex OAuth app-server process or a Telegram channel replay; this patch validates the source-proven app-server watchdog path directly.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `git diff --check`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | rg "extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt\.(ts|test\.ts)" || true`

## Audit

- Audit A (existing helper): existing `turnCompletionIdleWatchArmed`/`armTurnCompletionIdleWatch` path reused; no new predicate/helper introduced.
- Audit B (shared callers): `runCodexAppServerAttempt` is shared, but the change preserves the existing watchdog contract and only narrows one notification disarm case inside the local handler.
- Audit C (broader rival): #75213 is an old conflicting bot PR in the same harness area but targets unrelated shared-client traffic; #85242 has rival #85296 and was skipped. No direct active PR fixes #85251.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(discord): keep forced voice consult diagnostics private
target.body:
## Summary
- Stop forced Discord realtime consult fallbacks from appending internal consult-policy diagnostics to the agent-facing voice prompt.
- Apply the same private-diagnostic treatment to phone-call realtime forced consults while preserving the forced-consult reason in server debug logs.
- Add Discord voice regression coverage and update voice-call expectations; add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts extensions/discord/src/voice/manager.e2e.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts --reporter=verbose -t "forces an agent consult from final user transcript when consult policy is always"`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/discord/src/voice/realtime.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Realtime voice forced consult fallback no longer feeds internal consult-policy diagnostics into model-facing prompts that can be spoken back to users.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout with mocked Discord realtime voice and voice-call realtime provider harnesses.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the Vitest commands listed above after moving the patch onto a clean branch from origin/main; after the review-comment fix, reran the Discord e2e file, the focused voice-call forced-consult regression, oxfmt check, and `git diff --check`.
Evidence after fix: Discord regression asserts the agent command message is exactly `What?` and does not contain `consultPolicy` or `openclaw_agent_consult`; voice-call tests assert forced consult args include only the caller question; both forced fallback paths now log the private forced-consult reason through server debug logging instead of passing it to the model/tool payload.
Observed result after fix: Discord voice e2e file passed; voice-call forced-consult regression passed; diff whitespace check passed; touched files are oxfmt-clean; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings on the original patch.
What was not tested: A live Discord or phone call with real OpenAI realtime audio was not run.

target.title: fix(update): avoid broad tag fetches for dev updates
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw update --channel dev` could fail before updating `main` when a release tag on the remote conflicted with an existing local tag.
- Solution: make dev updates fetch branches/remotes with `--no-tags`, while keeping release-channel tag fetching unchanged.
- What changed: explicit dev tag targets now fetch only the requested tag from configured remotes before resolving it.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): stable/beta updates remain tag-driven and still fetch tags broadly.

## Motivation

- This fixes the observed beta-to-dev switch failure where an unrelated moved beta tag blocked a dev update that should track git `main`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw update --channel dev` no longer performs a broad tag fetch that can fail on unrelated moved release tags before updating from `main`.
- Real environment tested: Local bare git remote plus clean checkout reproducer, using real `git fetch` and `git rebase` commands with a moved conflicting `v2026.5.19-beta.2` tag.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a local bare remote, cloned it, moved the remote beta tag, confirmed current-main-style `git fetch --all --prune --tags` fails, then confirmed PR-style `git fetch --all --prune --no-tags` followed by `git rebase origin/main` succeeds.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
proof_root=<temp-redacted>

old dev broad tag fetch (current main behavior):
exit=1
From <temp-redacted>/remote
   c39fe2e..63b8b7c  main              -> origin/main
 ! [rejected]        v2026.5.19-beta.2 -> v2026.5.19-beta.2  (would clobber existing tag)

reset checkout to before proof fetch:
clean_status=0

new dev fetch + rebase (PR behavior):
local_tag_before=c39fe2e
remote_tag_after=63b8b7c
local_tag_after_no_tags_fetch=c39fe2e
head_matches_origin_main_after_rebase=yes

release-channel broad tag fetch remains tag-sensitive:
exit=1
From <temp-redacted>/remote
 ! [rejected]        v2026.5.19-beta.2 -> v2026.5.19-beta.2  (would clobber existing tag)
```

Supplemental focused test proof:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/update-runner.test.ts
Test Files 1 passed (1)
Tests 46 passed (46)
```

- Observed result after fix: The real git reproducer shows the old broad tag fetch fails with the same `would clobber existing tag` class of error, while the PR-style dev fetch avoids tag mutation and the checkout rebases to `origin/main`. A broad tag fetch still fails afterward, preserving the proof that release-channel tag-driven behavior remains tag-sensitive.
- What was not tested: A live openclaw-prod update was not run from this agent.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): User-reported failure showed `openclaw update --channel dev` rejected `v2026.5.19-beta.2` during broad tag fetch with `would clobber existing tag`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dev updates used the same broad tag-fetch behavior as release-channel updates even though dev is branch/main based.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Tests covered dev update fetch failures but did not assert that dev avoids broad tag fetching.
- Contributing context (if known): Switching from beta to dev can leave local release tags around, and Git rejects clobbering changed tags unless forced.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/update-runner.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Dev updates fetch with `--no-tags`; explicit dev tag targets fetch only the requested tag; failed targeted tag fetches do not fall back to stale local tags.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The update runner already injects the git command runner, so these tests verify the exact command contract without needing a mutable remote fixture.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing dev update tests covered fetch sequencing but not the tag-fetch mode.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw update --channel dev` now ignores unrelated remote tags during the normal branch update path. Explicit dev tag targets still fetch the requested tag.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
openclaw update --channel dev -> git fetch --all --prune --tags -> unrelated tag conflict -> update fails

After:
openclaw update --channel dev -> git fetch --all --prune --no-tags -> rebase main -> update continues
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The git fetch command changes for dev updates only. It narrows normal dev fetch behavior by suppressing tag fetches; explicit tag targets fetch only the requested tag ref.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux worktree
- Runtime/container: Node via repo test wrapper; local git bare remote proof
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): update CLI git mode
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Create a local bare git remote and clone it.
2. Create local tag `v2026.5.19-beta.2`, then move the remote tag to a newer commit.
3. Run current-main-style `git fetch --all --prune --tags` and observe tag-clobber failure.
4. Reset the checkout to clean state, run PR-style `git fetch --all --prune --no-tags`, then `git rebase origin/main`.
5. Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/update-runner.test.ts`.

### Expected

- Current-main-style broad tag fetch fails on the moved tag.
- PR-style dev fetch does not mutate/fetch the moved tag and branch rebase succeeds.
- Stable/beta paths continue to use tag fetching.
- Explicit dev tag targets do not use a stale local tag after targeted tag fetch failure.

### Actual

- Real git proof: broad tag fetch failed with `would clobber existing tag`; PR-style dev fetch plus rebase succeeded with `head_matches_origin_main_after_rebase=yes`.
- `src/infra/update-runner.test.ts` passed 46/46.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: real local bare-remote moved-tag reproduction, targeted update-runner tests, `git diff --check`, and `codex review --base origin/main` after rebase.
- Edge cases checked: default dev update fetch mode, explicit dev tag target, failed targeted tag fetch with a stale local tag candidate, release-channel broad tag sensitivity.
- What you did **not** verify: live update on openclaw-prod.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Explicit dev tag targets could otherwise resolve stale local tags after a failed targeted fetch.
  - Mitigation: The targeted tag fetch is forced for the requested ref and local tag resolution is skipped when all configured remote fetches fail.

target.title: fix(webchat): scope attachment button input
target.body:
## Summary
- route the visible WebChat attachment button to the file input inside its own composer instead of using a document-wide query
- mark the toolbar control as type=button so it cannot accidentally submit surrounding forms

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.
